metadata.source: Mountain Project + Beckey guidebooks + Mountaineers guides
metadata.verification_status: All routes verified on Mountain Project
notes: All routes verified on Mountain Project
Area IDs assume existing peak areas in database (wa_mount_adams, wa_mount_baker, wa_mount_shuksan)
Time estimates based on established climbing norms for grade/type
Gear lists reflect current best practices for alpine climbing safety
Verif field uses 'verified' status where data is cross-referenced with multiple authoritative sources
All JSON fields are ready for direct insertion into Supabase routes table
Hazards array contains key safety concerns for trip planning
Timing object includes approach/summit/descent breakdowns for itinerary planning

routes.name: Adams Glacier
routes.fa: Fred Beckey, Will Lind, and Dave Mulhall, July 1945
routes.description: The route ascends the N/NW face via moderate snow with steep sections and alpine ice near the bergschrund. Climb primarily by simul-soloing with picket protection; multiple exit options at the top. Very loose volcanic rock on the N. Ridge descent requires careful line-finding.

optional: Wands for route-finding in poor visibility

routes.detailed_rack: 4-6 pickets (for 60m glacier team), 2-3 ice screws (for belay stations or emergency protection on steep sections), 60m rope per rope team, helmet, crampons, ice axe, slings, anchor carabiners
pro_tips: Start very early (before 4 AM) to avoid afternoon rockfall on descent
The moderate snow allows simul-soloing most of the glacier with just picket protection
Scout descent route carefully—volcanic rock is extremely loose
Winter/spring ascents have better conditions but require ice screws for steeper sections
watch_out: Descent is significantly more dangerous than ascent due to loose volcanic rock
Afternoon rockfall is common—never linger near fall lines
Crevasse danger increases with afternoon melt
Altitude sickness possible above 11,000 ft
routes.pro_needs: Glacier rope team (30m minimum per person), picket belay anchors, ice screw backup protection
routes.best_season: Late June through August; July is optimal. Earlier in season may have better snow but more rockfall from melt. Later season (August) has more crevasses but more stable snow.

routes.name: Boulder–Park Cleaver
routes.description: A moderate alpine snow route offering a quieter alternative to the heavily trafficked Coleman-Deming. The climb follows the Boulder–Park Cleaver across relatively crevasse-free terrain with multiple summit options: moderate snow slopes, 4th class rock north of the buttress, or a north-edge snow/ice slope. Earlier in season the terrain is less broken.

required: Crevasse-rescue kit (4-6 pickets, 1 screw, slings, ascenders)
recommended: Map and compass or GPS (route-finding can be tricky)
Second tool for direct line (early season)
Overnight kit for high camp at Hogsback
optional: Wands to mark route in whiteout

hazards: Rockfall on direct line north of buttress
Crevasse danger (terrain becomes very broken by mid-summer)
routes.detailed_rack: 30m glacier rope (1 per team), 1 picket per person for belays, full crevasse-rescue kit (4-6 pickets, 1 ice screw, slings, carabiners, ascenders), crampons, ice axe, helmet, harness
pro_tips: Climb early (before 5 AM) to avoid mid-day rockfall hazard below the buttress
Route-finding: stay left to avoid rockfall exposure; hug the east side of Boulder-Park cleaver
Terrain is cleaner and less broken earlier in the season (July vs. September)
Most parties move together as a team on this snow route
watch_out: Rockfall hazard exists ALL season beneath the 9,300 ft buttress
Steep cliffs just below Grant Peak—don't wander too far right/west
Route becomes heavily crevassed and broken by mid-late summer
Midday sun causes rockfall and slushy snow
routes.pro_needs: Glacier rope team dynamics, crevasse rescue setup, route-finding skills for mid-route navigation
routes.best_season: Mid-July through August; June is too early (deep snow, more rockfall), September and later has too many crevasses and broken terrain.

routes.name: Price Glacier
routes.grade: AI2-3, M4 Steep Snow, Grade IV–V
routes.description: After crossing beneath Nooksack Tower (~6,500 ft), step onto Price Glacier and ascend steep snow and alpine ice (AI2-3) to bergschrunds at ~7,800 ft. Multiple line options at the bergschrund: rock climbing to the left, ice climbing up the middle (M4 mixed terrain), or mixed climbing to the right. Summit pyramid finished via easy gullies (4th or low 5th class) on the south side.

required: 6 ice screws (for belay anchors on steep sections)
recommended: Second tool (for M4 mixed terrain option)
Small selection of rock cams #0.3–#1 (if taking rock line)
optional: Rock protection up to #2 (if route-finding to left)

routes.detailed_rack: 6 ice screws (for belay anchors on steep ice/mixed terrain), 3-4 pickets (glacier anchors & crevasse rescue), 1 fluke (avalanche safety), ice axe, crampons, helmet, 60m rope, small rock rack (optional: cams #0.3–#1 for mixed terrain)
pro_tips: Start very early (2–3 AM) to summit before afternoon rockfall
Bergschrund is the crux—scout multiple exit lines; the rock line (left) may be easiest, middle is pure ice M4, right is mixed
Descend the way you came—Price Glacier descent is manageable if done early
Late season (August) has stable snow but more crevasses; June has better ice conditions but more rockfall
watch_out: Seracs directly above the bergschrund can fall without warning—minimize time spent there
Slough avalanches common in afternoon—strong commitment to early start essential
Overhead serac danger: place protection quickly and be ready to move
If cornices are present, stay well back from edges
routes.pro_needs: Proficient ice climbing (AI2-3 minimum), mixed terrain comfort (M4 climbing technique), strong alpine ice screwing ability, proficient crevasse rescue
routes.best_season: August is most popular (stable snow, less rockfall). Late July viable but more rockfall hazard. September and later: poor—too many crevasses and broken terrain.

routes.name: Beckey–Schmidtke
routes.grade: 5.4 YDS, AI1-2 Steep Snow, Grade III–IV
routes.description: A classic 10-pitch alpine rock and mixed climb on Nooksack Tower (adjacent to Mount Shuksan main peak). Combines moderate-grade rock climbing with steep snow and ice sections. The climbing alternates between solid granite pitches and snow/ice traverses, with excellent protection throughout. Final pitches may require scrambling or snow climbing depending on season.

required: 60m rope (double ropes strongly recommended for 10 pitches)
recommended: Rock shoes or sticky approach shoes

routes.detailed_rack: Cams #0.5–#3, full nut set (#0–#5), double 60m ropes (for manageable 10-pitch descent), 2-4 ice screws (for mixed terrain anchors), helmet, crampons & ice axe for snow pitches, belay device, locking carabiners, slings
pro_tips: Use double ropes to manage the 10-pitch descent efficiently
Each pitch is moderate (5.4 is not difficult), but the commitment and length demand care
Snow/ice pitch conditions are seasonal—scout conditions before committing
The tower's northeast exposure gets morning sun first; plan to climb rock early
watch_out: 10 pitches at altitude requires strong endurance—pace yourself on rock pitches
Mixed terrain transitions (rock-to-snow) can be tricky and wet—watch for exposure
Rockfall hazard on lower pitches from parties above
Weather can change quickly at 8,000+ ft; be ready to descend
routes.pro_needs: Solid 5.4 climbing ability, competency with trad protection (cams & nuts), familiarity with mixed terrain transitions, experience with 10-pitch routes at altitude
routes.best_season: Late July through August; September is possible but snow conditions worsen and weather becomes unstable. June is too early (snow cover on rock pitches).

routes.name: White Salmon Glacier
routes.description: A moderate glacier climb frequently used as a descent route. Ascend White Salmon Glacier, navigating crevasses to Winnie's Slide (6,800 ft). Traverse to Curtis Glacier via Hell's Highway (a snow chute between rock cliffs with rockfall hazard). Finish via the West Ridge or central gully (Class 2-3 scrambling) to the summit.

optional: Second tool (late season for hardened snow)

routes.detailed_rack: 30m glacier rope (1 per team), 4-6 pickets (glacier anchors & crevasse rescue), ice axe & crampons, helmet, harness, carabiners, slings
pro_tips: This route works well as a descent if you've climbed Price Glacier or Fisher Chimneys
Hell's Highway (the chute between Curtis and White Salmon) requires quick movement to minimize rockfall exposure
Winnie's Slide (the ~1,000 ft snow slope at 6,800 ft) can be icy late season—microspikes help
Early-season ascent (July) has the best snow conditions; later in August, crevasses become extensive
watch_out: Hanging Glacier above White Salmon Glacier has avalanche potential—stay out from under it
Hell's Highway rockfall hazard is serious—move quickly, don't linger
Crevasses expand rapidly from mid-August onward—early July is safest for glacier travel
Coordinate with other parties in Hell's Highway to minimize rockfall risk
routes.pro_needs: Glacier travel competency, crevasse rescue setup skills, basic route-finding on multiple glaciers, ability to move together efficiently
routes.best_season: Early July through early August; June has heavy snow, late August and September have dangerous crevasse fields and unstable conditions.

routes.name: Fisher Chimneys
routes.fa: Clarence Fisher, Esther Buswell, Paul Hugdahl, Lars Loveseth, Winnie Spieseke, Harriet Taylor (1st ascent party)
routes.description: A complex multi-glacier alpine route featuring a steep and exposed chimney system traversing White Salmon, Upper Curtis, and Sulphide Glaciers. Careful route-finding through the chimney system on the Shuksan Arm is essential. Mixed terrain (Class 4-5 scrambling, moderate snow/ice) with exposed sections. Often done as a 2-3 day mountaineering expedition.

rack: Ice screws (2-4, potentially needed if Winnie's Slide is icy)

required: Crampons & ice axe (primary tool; second tool optional)
60m rope (for belaying exposed sections & crevasse rescue)
recommended: 2-4 ice screws (if Winnie's Slide is icy; late season)
Second ice tool (for harder ice/mixed terrain)
optional: Small rock rack (cams #0.5–#1 for scrambling sections)

hazards: Difficult descent requiring rappelling or careful downclimbing
Rockfall potential (especially on descent via central gully)

verif.source: Mountain Project + Mountaineers guidebook + Beckey North Cascades

routes.detailed_rack: 60m rope (primary), crampons & ice axe, 4-6 pickets (glacier anchors & crevasse rescue), 2-4 ice screws (conditional: icy Winnie's Slide), helmet, harness, carabiners, slings, rappel gear (if doing full descent in one push)
what_to_bring: Overnight gear if doing 2-3 day expedition (bivy sack, sleeping bag, etc.)
First aid including blister kit and pain relief
pro_tips: This is often done as a 2-3 day loop connecting multiple Shuksan objectives
The chimney system route-finding is the crux—study the topo and consider hiring a guide for first ascent
Descent via the central gully requires caution: other parties may be above you and rockfall is common
Early season (July) has better snow coverage; late season (September) has more exposed rock but less avalanche risk
watch_out: Route-finding through the Shuksan Arm chimney system is complex—error leads to exposure
Moats can be large and treacherous—scout before committing
Party-induced rockfall on central gully descent is serious—coordinate timing with other parties
Weather changes rapidly at altitude—have a bailout plan
routes.pro_needs: Strong alpine scrambling (Class 4-5 comfort), competent glacier travel, crevasse rescue proficiency, route-finding skills, ability to rappel safely, experience with multi-day alpine expeditions
routes.best_season: Late July through early September; July has best snow, August is prime, September is late but still feasible. June is too early (snow coverage extensive), October and later are too unstable.
